summary: The session focused on the challenges faced by African Americans during the Great Migration, including housing and job discrimination, racial segregation, police brutality, and cultural prejudice. The user expressed interest in the organizations and leaders that emerged to support African Americans during this period and sought ways to contribute to the ongoing fight for racial justice.
facts: African Americans faced housing discrimination, job discrimination, racial segregation, police brutality, and cultural prejudice during the Great Migration. The National Association for the Advancement of Colored People (NAACP) was founded in 1909 to end racial discrimination and segregation through legal action. The National Urban League was established in 1910 to promote economic and social equality for African Americans in urban areas. Marcus Garvey founded the Universal Negro Improvement Association (UNIA) in 1914 to advocate for black economic independence and self-determination. W.E.B. Du Bois co-founded the NAACP in 1909 and promoted racial equality and challenged white supremacy. A. Philip Randolph founded the Brotherhood of Sleeping Car Porters, the first predominantly African American labor union in the United States. The efforts of leaders and organizations during the Great Migration laid the groundwork for the civil rights movement in the 1950s and 1960s. Individuals can support racial justice by educating themselves, amplifying voices of people of color, donating to organizations, speaking up against racism, and advocating for policies that promote racial justice.
